merge_policy.threshold: merge documents when TF-IDF cosine similarity >= 0.60 OR 5-token shingle containment of the smaller document >= 0.60
merge_policy.why_containment_is_used: Containment catches shorter PDFs that are substantially embedded inside longer PDFs even when whole-document Jaccard/cosine is lower.

From the ADHD Experts at
The Professionals Who Diagnose & Treat ADHD A Comparative Guide A trusted source of advice and information for families touched by attention-deficit disorder— and a voice of inspiration to help people with ADHD find success at home, at school, and on the job.
ADDitudeMag.com

LEGAL NOTICES ADDitude does not provide medical advice, diagnosis, or treatment. The information offered here is for informa-
tional purposes only and is not intended to substitute or replace professional medical advice, diagnosis, or treat-
ment. Always consult with your physician or other quali-
fied health-care professional or educational consultant for questions about your health and education, or the health and education of your child and family members. Do not disregard, avoid, or delay obtaining medical or health related advice from your health-care professional because of something you have read here.
If you think you may have a medical emergency, call your doctor or 911 immediately. ADDitude does not recom-
mend or endorse any specific tests, physicians, products, procedures, opinions, or other information that may be mentioned in ADDitude publications or websites. Reliance on any information provided by ADDitude is solely at your own risk.

What do MDs, PhDs, and LCSWs share in common? All of these medical professionals can treat attention deficit disorder (ADHD or ADD) — but which ones provide the treatment services you need, nearby, and at an af-
fordable price? Answering those questions — and sorting out the soup of professional acronyms — can get really overwhelming, really fast. Here, we aim to simplify the process with a comparative chart that high-
lights the pros and cons of 14 different types of ADHD professionals. Use this to better understand your options and make the right decisions about your family’s mental health care.
The good news: Treatment options for attention deficit disorder have multiplied exponentially in the last two decades. The bad news? Choosing the most affordable and effective strategy is overwhelming at best, and downright daunting for many patients and families living with ADHD. Who can prescribe medication? Who can administer diagnostic tests? Who can offer counseling? Who is available for more than 15 minutes at a time? Read on to learn the pros and cons of PCPs, psychologists, counselors, and many other ADHD professionals.
By the ADDitude Editors
The Professionals Who Diagnose & Treat ADHD: A Comparative Guide 4
Treatment Provider
Advantages
Disadvantages
Family Doctor or Primary Care Physician (PCP)
A medical doctor responsible for your overall health care; the first place many people go when they first suspect symptoms of ADHD
• Familiar with your or your child’s medical history
• Covered by insurance
• Can diagnose ADHD and prescribe medication
• No referral required; easy to get an appointment
• Seldom have in-depth knowledge of ADHD symptoms or treatment
• May focus on medication management
• Brief office visits may lead to rushed diagnosis and/or treatment
Developmental and Behavioral Pediatrician
A medical doctor who has additional training in learning disabilities, developmental disorders, and other behavioral challenges
• Can provide a more specialized evaluation than most PCPs
• Can diagnose and treat related conditions effectively
• Often covered by insurance
• Can prescribe medication
• Appointments may be tough to snag, particularly in underserved areas
• May need to refer out for more specialized testing
Psychiatrist
A medical doctor who specializes in psychiatry; has completed medical school and received education and training in diagnosing and treating mental health disorders like ADHD, depression, and anxiety
• Understands the relationship between emotional and physical disorders
• Can prescribe medication; may provide talk therapy as well
• Adept at diagnosing and treating comorbid conditions
• Covered by most insurance plans
• May focus on medication management and refer out for talk therapy
• Copays often more expensive than are those for PCPs
Psychopharmacologist A medical doctor (either an M.D., psychiatrist, or Doctor of Osteopathic Medicine) who specializes in the medications used to treat mental and emotional disorders
• Often covered by insurance
• Adept at prescribing multiple medications for ADHD and comorbid conditions
• May serve as a consultant to another specialist, and not meet with you directly 5
Treatment Provider
Advantages
Disadvantages
Psychologist
Has an advanced degree in psychology (Ph.D. or Psy.D), and can provide talk therapy to patients in a clinical environment
• Can diagnose ADHD and related conditions
• Often easier to see than a psychiatrist
• Usually covered by insurance
• Helps patients manage the emotional challenges of life with ADHD
• Can help educate patients about ADHD symptoms and treatment options
• In most states, cannot prescribe medication (some exceptions)
• Must refer out to a psychiatrist or PCP for medication management
Licensed Professional Counselor (LPC) or Licensed Mental Health Counselor (LMHC)
Professionals who have been licensed by a state board to provide counseling-based mental therapy
• Qualified to diagnose ADHD and most related conditions
• Can provide counseling, including marriage and family counseling
• Usually less expensive than other mental health professionals
• Often covered by insurance
• Cannot prescribe medication
• May prefer to refer out for more in-depth diagnostic assessments
Neurologist
A medical doctor who specializes in disorders of the brain, spinal cord, and nervous system
• Adept at diagnosing seizure disorders, Tourette Syndrome, or other related brain-based conditions that often appear with ADHD
• Can perform brain scans to rule out certain conditions
• Can prescribe medication
• Not always covered by insurance
• May be expensive, particularly when brain scans are performed
• EEGs, MRIs, and other brain scans are not necessary to diagnose ADHD itself
Neuropsychologist
Focuses on a relatively young branch of psychology, and studies how specific structures of the brain relate to psychological disorders and patterns of behavior
• Can make recommendations about school placement
• Can help children and adults plan out and access multiple treatment interventions
• Can fully assess cognitive and behavioral functioning, and provide differential diagnoses
• Not usually covered by insurance
• May need to refer out for talk therapy, medication management, or other services
• Cannot prescribe medication 6
Treatment Provider
Advantages
Disadvantages
Psychiatric-Mental Health Nurse (PMHN)
A nurse who has undergone additional specialized training to work in psychiatric hospitals and treatment centers
• Can assess the mental health needs of individuals, families, and groups
• Adept at crisis intervention
• Serves as a patient advocate
• Often covered by insurance
• Most cannot diagnose ADHD or prescribe medication (see APRNs, below)
• Usually works in concert with doctors and hospitals, not independently
Psychiatric Advanced Practice Registered Nurse (APRN)
Psychiatric mental health nurses who have obtained a master’s or doctoral degree in the field
• Can diagnose ADHD and related conditions
• State laws vary, but can usually prescribe medication and/or provide therapy
• Some studies show patient satisfaction on par with primary care doctors
• Usually covered by insurance
• Depending on state, may have limited prescription abilities or require a physician’s supervision
Nurse Practitioner or Psychiatric Nurse Practitioner
A type of APRN who has pursued the further education and specialization needed to serve as a primary care provider for children and adults
• Usually able to diagnose ADHD and related conditions
• Can devise a complete treatment plan and provide therapy
• Can prescribe medication
• Covered by insurance
• Often more available via appointment than are other mental health professionals
• In some small studies, NPs outscored physicians in patient satisfaction
• Depending on state, may need to collaborate with a physician to prescribe medication
• May have limited knowledge of ADHD or related conditions, depending on training 7
Treatment Provider
Advantages
Disadvantages
Licensed Clinical Social Worker (LCSW)
A professional who has obtained a Master’s degree (or higher) in social work and has been licensed by a state board to provide mental health services
• Able to diagnose ADHD and most related conditions
• Excels at providing emotional support to patients and families
• Adheres to high ethical standards for patient care
• Often costs less than a psychiatrist or psychologist
• Usually covered by insurance
• Cannot prescribe medication
• Depending on training, may struggle with complex diagnoses
Occupational Therapist (OT)
A licensed professional — usually with a Master’s degree or higher — who helps children and adults with motor deficits, sensory processing challenges, and underdeveloped social skills to function better in day-to-day life
• Can diagnose and treat ADHD, SPD, dysgraphia, and many other comorbid conditions
• Uses a unique, hands-on therapy that provides motor and sensory benefits
• May be accessed through your child’s school
• May also offer home visits
• Usually more beneficial for children than for adults
• Insurance often only covers OT for certain diagnoses
• In the U.S., demand exceeds supply for occupational therapy • Cannot prescribe medication
ADHD Coach
Not a medical professional, but a guide, teacher, and cheerleader who helps teach organizational, time-management, and goal-achieving strategies to teens and adults with ADHD
• Helps clients build lifestyle skills (organization, time management, etc.) that are not often covered by medical professionals
• Can guide clients toward specific life goals
• Many have ADHD — and a unique perspective on the condition
• No nationwide standards for licensing coaches currently exist
• Can be expensive; rarely covered by insurance
• Cannot diagnose ADHD or prescribe medication ADDitude eBooks Available Now
additudemag.com/shop
ADHD Medication and Treatment
The latest information on managing medication, starting therapy, evalu-
ating alternative treatments, and more.
You’re relieved to know, finally, that your lifelong symptoms are due to ADHD. But now, you have questions — on everything from which medica-
tions are available to how to tell if they’re working properly. In this compre-
hensive special report, you’ll learn how to seek an accurate diagnosis and map out a treatment plan that’s right for you. >> Learn more about this eBook: http://additu.de/treatment
Your Complete ADHD Guide
A complete overview of ADHD, outlining every step from diagnosis to treatment — all the way to living successfully with attention deficit.
From the moment you suspect ADHD in yourself or your child, you have hundreds of questions. Which doctors can evaluate symptoms? What medi-
cation side effects should you be prepared for? Can diet help? This compre-
hensive eBook has over 100 pages of expert advice, personal stories, and more to help you become an ADHD expert. >> Learn more about this eBook: http://additu.de/adhd-101
Mindfulness and Other Natural Treatments
The best non-medical treatments for ADHD, including exercise, green time, and mindful meditation.
Learn how mindfulness works on ADHD brains, and how to begin practic-
ing it today. Plus, research the benefits of other alternative treatments like yoga and deep breathing exercises — including some designed especially for kids — as well as the science behind each natural therapy.
>> Learn more about this eBook: http://additu.de/mindful
FREE ADDitude Downloads
13 Questions to Ask Before Starting ADHD Medication
The best questions to ask your doctor before starting or tweaking any ADHD medication regimen.
It’s Not ADHD: 3 Common Diagnosis Mistakes
Doctors are sometimes too quick to diagnose ADHD. Read up on common misdiagnoses.
11 ADHD Coping Mechanisms Dr. William Dodson’s 11 ADHD-
tested treatment strategies that really work..
Secrets of the ADHD Brain
Learn why we think, act, and feel the way we do..
ADHD Vitamins and Supplements
Find out which herbs, vitamins, and nutritional supplements may help control some symptoms of ADHD.
9 Foods to Eat (And Avoid) for Improved ADHD Symptoms
Your guide to a high-protein, low-
sugar, no-additive diet.
The Essential ADHD Monitoring System
How to know if your medication is working — and how to catch warning signs before they become problems..
Find these and many more free ADHD resources online at:
http://additu.de/
freedownloads Expert Webinar Replays: Cracking the ADHD Medication Maze
>> http://additu.de/med-maze
Roughly 80 percent of all people with ADHD benefit from taking medication. Still, many patients never get to experience their effectiveness. Why? Finding prescribers, untangling insurance, dealing with uncooperative pharmacists, and more ADHD-unfriendly obstacles get in the way. In this webinar, learn insider strategies for finding — and tweaking — the medication that works best for you.
Emotions and ADHD: What Clinicians Need to Know
>> http://additu.de/adhdemotions
Emotional challenges are one of the core problems of ADHD, but too many clinicians — and even some self-proclaimed ADHD experts — don’t know what to look for or mistake emotional outbursts as symptoms of other con-
ditions. In this expert webinar, William Dodson, M.D., breaks down how ADHD impacts emotions, and outlines what professionals can do to help pa-
tients manage out-of-control feelings.
Healing the ADHD Brain: Interventions and Strategies that Work
>> http://additu.de/healing
There’s no one-size-fits-all approach for treating ADHD. Here, Daniel G. Amen, M.D., explains treatment options ranging from medication and supplements to diet and exercise.
Neurofeedback and Cognitive Training for Kids
>> http://additu.de/neuro
Is brain training — including neurofeedback and cognitive training (CT) — really all it’s cracked up to be? Is it safe for kids? Is it worth the money? Naomi Steiner, M.D., shares the research behind some computer-based al-
ternative therapies so you can make an informed decision before treating your child.
The ADHD-Executive Function Connection
>> http://additu.de/efunction
We’ve all heard of executive functions — the brain-based skills that affect how we plan, organize, and carry out tasks. But how do executive functions relate to ADHD — and how can children and adults with the condition com-
pensate for natural deficiencies in these critical skills? Thomas Brown, Ph.D., explains the ins and outs of executive functions and how they affect your focus in this expert webinar.
FREE ADHD Newsletters from ADDitude
Sign up to receive criti-
cal news and information about ADHD diagnosis and treatment, success at school, adult ADHD, and parenting strategies: http://additu.de/newsletter
Adult ADHD and LD
Expert advice on managing your household, time, money, career, and relationships
Parenting Children with ADHD and LD Strategies and support for par-
ents on behavior and discipline, time management, disorganiza-
tion, and making friends.
ADHD and LD at School How to get classroom accom-
modations, finish homework, work with teachers, find the right schools, and much more.
Treating ADHD Treatment options for attention deficit including medications, food, supplements, brain training, mindfulness and other alternative therapies.
For Women with ADHD Managing ADHD on the job, run-
ning a household, dealing with challenging emotions, and much more.

in the medications used
to treat mental and
emotional disorders
• Often covered by insurance

• Adept at prescribing multiple
medications for ADHD and
comorbid conditions

• May serve as a consultant to
another specialist, and not
meet with you directly

pages.text:
5
Treatment Provider
Advantages
Disadvantages
Psychologist
Has an advanced degree in psychology (Ph.D. or Psy.D), and can provide talk therapy to patients in a clinical environment
• Can diagnose ADHD and related conditions
• Often easier to see than a psychiatrist
• Usually covered by insurance
• Helps patients manage the emotional challenges of life with ADHD
• Can help educate patients about ADHD symptoms and treatment options
• In most states, cannot prescribe medication (some exceptions)
• Must refer out to a psychiatrist or PCP for medication management
Licensed Professional Counselor (LPC) or Licensed Mental Health Counselor (LMHC)
Professionals who have been licensed by a state board to provide counseling-based mental therapy
• Qualified to diagnose ADHD and most related conditions
• Can provide counseling, including marriage and family counseling
• Usually less expensive than other mental health professionals
• Often covered by insurance
• Cannot prescribe medication
• May prefer to refer out for more in-depth diagnostic assessments
Neurologist
A medical doctor who specializes in disorders of the brain, spinal cord, and nervous system
• Adept at diagnosing seizure disorders, Tourette Syndrome, or other related brain-based conditions that often appear with ADHD
• Can perform brain scans to rule out certain conditions
• Can prescribe medication
• Not always covered by insurance
• May be expensive, particularly when brain scans are performed
• EEGs, MRIs, and other brain scans are not necessary to diagnose ADHD itself
Neuropsychologist
Focuses on a relatively young branch of psychology, and studies how specific structures of the brain relate to psychological disorders and patterns of behavior
• Can make recommendations about school placement
• Can help children and adults plan out and access multiple treatment interventions
• Can fully assess cognitive and behavioral functioning, and provide differential diagnoses
• Not usually covered by insurance
• May need to refer out for talk therapy, medication management, or other services
• Cannot prescribe medication

pages.text:
6
Treatment Provider
Advantages
Disadvantages
Psychiatric-Mental Health Nurse (PMHN)
A nurse who has undergone additional specialized training to work in psychiatric hospitals and treatment centers
• Can assess the mental health needs of individuals, families, and groups
• Adept at crisis intervention
• Serves as a patient advocate
• Often covered by insurance
• Most cannot diagnose ADHD or prescribe medication (see APRNs, below)
• Usually works in concert with doctors and hospitals, not independently
Psychiatric Advanced Practice Registered Nurse (APRN)
Psychiatric mental health nurses who have obtained a master’s or doctoral degree in the field
• Can diagnose ADHD and related conditions
• State laws vary, but can usually prescribe medication and/or provide therapy
• Some studies show patient satisfaction on par with primary care doctors
• Usually covered by insurance
• Depending on state, may have limited prescription abilities or require a physician’s supervision
Nurse Practitioner or Psychiatric Nurse Practitioner
A type of APRN who has pursued the further education and specialization needed to serve as a primary care provider for children and adults
• Usually able to diagnose ADHD and related conditions
• Can devise a complete treatment plan and provide therapy
• Can prescribe medication
• Covered by insurance
• Often more available via appointment than are other mental health professionals
• In some small studies, NPs outscored physicians in patient satisfaction
• Depending on state, may need to collaborate with a physician to prescribe medication
• May have limited knowledge of ADHD or related conditions, depending on training

Health Nurse (PMHN)

or Psychiatric Nurse
Practitioner

A type of APRN who
has pursued the
further education and

provider for children and

and related conditions

pages.text:
7
Treatment Provider
Advantages
Disadvantages
Licensed Clinical Social Worker (LCSW)
A professional who has obtained a Master’s degree (or higher) in social work and has been licensed by a state board to provide mental health services
• Able to diagnose ADHD and most related conditions
• Excels at providing emotional support to patients and families
• Adheres to high ethical standards for patient care
• Often costs less than a psychiatrist or psychologist
• Usually covered by insurance
• Cannot prescribe medication
• Depending on training, may struggle with complex diagnoses
Occupational Therapist (OT)
A licensed professional — usually with a Master’s degree or higher — who helps children and adults with motor deficits, sensory processing challenges, and underdeveloped social skills to function better in day-to-day life
• Can diagnose and treat ADHD, SPD, dysgraphia, and many other comorbid conditions
• Uses a unique, hands-on therapy that provides motor and sensory benefits
• May be accessed through your child’s school
• May also offer home visits
• Usually more beneficial for children than for adults
• Insurance often only covers OT for certain diagnoses
• In the U.S., demand exceeds supply for occupational therapy • Cannot prescribe medication
ADHD Coach
Not a medical professional, but a guide, teacher, and cheerleader who helps teach organizational, time-management, and goal-achieving strategies to teens and adults with ADHD
• Helps clients build lifestyle skills (organization, time management, etc.) that are not often covered by medical professionals
• Can guide clients toward specific life goals
• Many have ADHD — and a unique perspective on the condition
• No nationwide standards for licensing coaches currently exist
• Can be expensive; rarely covered by insurance
• Cannot diagnose ADHD or prescribe medication

pages.text:
ADDitude eBooks Available Now
additudemag.com/shop
ADHD Medication and Treatment
The latest information on managing medication, starting therapy, evalu-
ating alternative treatments, and more.
You’re relieved to know, finally, that your lifelong symptoms are due to ADHD. But now, you have questions — on everything from which medica-
tions are available to how to tell if they’re working properly. In this compre-
hensive special report, you’ll learn how to seek an accurate diagnosis and map out a treatment plan that’s right for you. >> Learn more about this eBook: http://additu.de/treatment
Your Complete ADHD Guide
A complete overview of ADHD, outlining every step from diagnosis to treatment — all the way to living successfully with attention deficit.
From the moment you suspect ADHD in yourself or your child, you have hundreds of questions. Which doctors can evaluate symptoms? What medi-
cation side effects should you be prepared for? Can diet help? This compre-
hensive eBook has over 100 pages of expert advice, personal stories, and more to help you become an ADHD expert. >> Learn more about this eBook: http://additu.de/adhd-101
Mindfulness and Other Natural Treatments
The best non-medical treatments for ADHD, including exercise, green time, and mindful meditation.
Learn how mindfulness works on ADHD brains, and how to begin practic-
ing it today. Plus, research the benefits of other alternative treatments like yoga and deep breathing exercises — including some designed especially for kids — as well as the science behind each natural therapy.
>> Learn more about this eBook: http://additu.de/mindful
FREE ADDitude Downloads
13 Questions to Ask Before Starting ADHD Medication
The best questions to ask your doctor before starting or tweaking any ADHD medication regimen.
It’s Not ADHD: 3 Common Diagnosis Mistakes
Doctors are sometimes too quick to diagnose ADHD. Read up on common misdiagnoses.
11 ADHD Coping Mechanisms Dr. William Dodson’s 11 ADHD-
tested treatment strategies that really work..
Secrets of the ADHD Brain
Learn why we think, act, and feel the way we do..
ADHD Vitamins and Supplements
Find out which herbs, vitamins, and nutritional supplements may help control some symptoms of ADHD.
9 Foods to Eat (And Avoid) for Improved ADHD Symptoms
Your guide to a high-protein, low-
sugar, no-additive diet.
The Essential ADHD Monitoring System
How to know if your medication is working — and how to catch warning signs before they become problems..
Find these and many more free ADHD resources online at:
http://additu.de/
freedownloads

pages.text:
Expert Webinar Replays: Cracking the ADHD Medication Maze
>> http://additu.de/med-maze
Roughly 80 percent of all people with ADHD benefit from taking medication. Still, many patients never get to experience their effectiveness. Why? Finding prescribers, untangling insurance, dealing with uncooperative pharmacists, and more ADHD-unfriendly obstacles get in the way. In this webinar, learn insider strategies for finding — and tweaking — the medication that works best for you.
Emotions and ADHD: What Clinicians Need to Know
>> http://additu.de/adhdemotions
Emotional challenges are one of the core problems of ADHD, but too many clinicians — and even some self-proclaimed ADHD experts — don’t know what to look for or mistake emotional outbursts as symptoms of other con-
ditions. In this expert webinar, William Dodson, M.D., breaks down how ADHD impacts emotions, and outlines what professionals can do to help pa-
tients manage out-of-control feelings.
Healing the ADHD Brain: Interventions and Strategies that Work
>> http://additu.de/healing
There’s no one-size-fits-all approach for treating ADHD. Here, Daniel G. Amen, M.D., explains treatment options ranging from medication and supplements to diet and exercise.
Neurofeedback and Cognitive Training for Kids
>> http://additu.de/neuro
Is brain training — including neurofeedback and cognitive training (CT) — really all it’s cracked up to be? Is it safe for kids? Is it worth the money? Naomi Steiner, M.D., shares the research behind some computer-based al-
ternative therapies so you can make an informed decision before treating your child.
The ADHD-Executive Function Connection
>> http://additu.de/efunction
We’ve all heard of executive functions — the brain-based skills that affect how we plan, organize, and carry out tasks. But how do executive functions relate to ADHD — and how can children and adults with the condition com-
pensate for natural deficiencies in these critical skills? Thomas Brown, Ph.D., explains the ins and outs of executive functions and how they affect your focus in this expert webinar.
FREE ADHD Newsletters from ADDitude
Sign up to receive criti-
cal news and information about ADHD diagnosis and treatment, success at school, adult ADHD, and parenting strategies: http://additu.de/newsletter
Adult ADHD and LD
Expert advice on managing your household, time, money, career, and relationships
Parenting Children with ADHD and LD Strategies and support for par-
ents on behavior and discipline, time management, disorganiza-
tion, and making friends.
ADHD and LD at School How to get classroom accom-
modations, finish homework, work with teachers, find the right schools, and much more.
Treating ADHD Treatment options for attention deficit including medications, food, supplements, brain training, mindfulness and other alternative therapies.
For Women with ADHD Managing ADHD on the job, run-
ning a household, dealing with challenging emotions, and much more.

ourspace_kindness_pass.title: pdf_to_json_text_layout_v1

main_doability_needs: bed-level or seated options by default
oxygen/breathing, pain, vertigo, fatigue, mobility, and recovery time respected
ADHD/autism supports: transitions, low-stimulation starts, tiny visible steps
partial completion and stopping before a crash count as success
no shame, no body blame, no pressure to push through pain or dysphoria
ADHD transition support before task demands
caregiver burnout recovery treated as care, not avoidance
protected rest, boundaries, decompression, sensory needs, and people-pleasing checks
no assumption that Jasper has unlimited caregiver capacity

household_rules: no hour block may hold more than two tasks/tools
skills may be used as one-breath or one-field versions
survival days load critical care first and skip optional worksheets
rest, boundaries, decompression, and asking for help count as care
skills are for support, not grades

universal_kindness_rules: This is a support tool, not a test.
One line, one checkbox, one breath, or one tiny step counts.
Skipping a skill because the body is unsafe, exhausted, dysregulated, or overloaded is allowed.
Do not stack this with more than one other task inside the same hour.
Survival-day version always wins over perfection.
william_accessibility_adaptations: Use bed-level, seated, voice-note, or caregiver-assisted versions when needed.
Avoid pain escalation, respiratory strain, unsafe transfers, and dysphoria-triggering language.
Break worksheets into one-field chunks; stopping before a crash is success.
jasper_accessibility_adaptations: Start with transition support before expecting focus.
Protect caregiver burnout recovery; rest and boundaries are valid completion.
Use body-doubling, timers, low-stimulation versions, and one-minute starts.
tiny_steps: Use a timer, body double, or transition cue.
Stop after one tiny step if energy drops.

routine_integration.routine_role: yearly reference, review, or archive check
routine_integration.start_the_day_use: not auto-loaded; use for annual care-plan / docs review
routine_integration.recommended_block: yearly OurSpace maintenance and support refresh
